narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 67.4% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction draws from patterns in academic records, test scores, and backgrounds of past students, focusing on key indicators of bar success without considering gender or race. In your case, your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, as it falls well below the typical fifth or sixth decile among those who passed. Similarly, your second-decile ranking in Year 1 grades reinforced this concern, signaling early academic challenges compared to the stronger sixth-decile averages of successful bar takers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.70 actually worked in your favor, exceeding the 3.24 average of those who passed and pulling the prediction somewhat toward success. Your LSAT score of 31, however, lagged behind the 37.27 average of passers, contributing to the overall risk profile, while your middle family income aligns with about a third of successful students, and your full-time study plans match the norm for 93% of them. Compared to typical admitted students, who generally show higher year-end deciles and LSATs alongside solid GPAs, your profile highlighted vulnerabilities in those school rankings that outweighed your GPA strength. The model prioritizes these academic deciles because they have proven to be the most reliable predictors of bar outcomes in similar cases. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven flag on early performance trends that have tripped up others. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and if possible, strengthen your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program that demonstrates upward momentum. Many students in your shoes have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so reviewing your Year 1 and 3 transcripts for patterns and seeking targeted tutoring could reshape your next application powerfully. You're clearly capable, given that standout GPA, and with focused improvements, a future prediction could easily flip in your favor.
